cards.back: The degree to which elements of a class are related to its purpose. High cohesion = each class does one job well. Low cohesion = class does many unrelated things.
The degree of dependency between classes. Low coupling = classes depend on abstractions, not internals. High coupling = tight dependencies, hard to change.
A class should have one reason to change. One job, one responsibility. If a class does many things, split it into smaller, focused classes.
Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. Add new behavior via inheritance, not by changing existing code.
Child classes must be substitutable for parent classes. Any child can be used wherever the parent is expected without breaking the program.
Don't force classes to implement methods they don't use. Create smaller, focused interfaces. Clients depend only on what they need.
Depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Allows swapping implementations easily and improves testability via mocking.
An anti-pattern: a class that knows and does everything. Hard to understand, test, reuse, and maintain. Solution: split by responsibility.
Improving code structure without changing its behavior. Common example: breaking a God class into smaller, focused classes.
An acronym for 5 design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
Passing dependencies to a class constructor instead of creating them internally. Enables testability and loose coupling.
A contract defining what methods a class must implement. In Python, abstract classes (ABC) define interfaces. Enables polymorphism.
